text:
It is no myth that the old motel by the river was a fantastic place, eminently lovable despite its problems. Each room was an oblong box with a nylon curtain, a single plate, and a boiler that would screech like jazz when you turned the meter past forty watts. The owner, a clerk with a hairy chin and partial deafness, liked to loll behind the desk and carry on a long discourse about citizenship, nationality, and the status of every guest who would enter.

"Goodness, what a treasure of a county this is," he would say, vainly waving a brochure like a placard. He had a mania for psychology and would allude to calculus and equilibrium as if every problem in life could be subdivided and solved. As an example, he insisted that the density of maple trees by the river was due to some cosmic policy, a point he made with comic horror. His argument would bend and slip until it became abortive, and the annoyance was real, yet his manner was so agreeable you could not mock him.

A messenger arrived one March morning, sent to allot rooms to a flock of tourists. A swarm of adolescent travelers began to hustle and squat in the lobby, eager to develop their plans. One had the mumps; the staff feared it might infect the others, so they kept that calf-eyed boy apart. The conductor of their tour, who would insure the group and entrust their bags to a porter, asked the owner to orientate them. "You must tend to your luggage," the owner said, tagging each kilo of baggage as the guests would interact and trade tips.

The alternation of sun and rain over the river gave the place a charm that no closedown could erase. After a mishap with a leaking pipe, the owner did not stoop to blame anyone; he simply set out a tray of mint liqueur to tempt his guests and exhibit his hospitality. The dip in the road, the large maple shading the porch, the rare and infrequent peace of it all comprise a picture I treasure. No breach of trust, no martyr's complaint could spoil that retention of memory. To this very day the capability of that humble inn to ship a weary soul toward calm feels almost holy. My old companion still keeps a phrase from that place tagged on a card: "No watt of worry, no grill of fear, can spoil a quiet river." My companion and I would grill fish on the porch each night, watching a single watt of light flicker; I learned to subdivide the bill and tag each item, repeating the phrase he taught me with no fear at all.
translation:
河边那家老旅馆是个奇妙的地方，这绝非神话；尽管它毛病不少，却极其讨人喜爱。每个房间都是一只长方形的盒子，配着尼龙窗帘、一只盘子，还有一台锅炉，当你把电表调到四十瓦以上时，它就会像爵士乐一样尖叫。店主是个下巴长着硬毛、有部分耳聋的职员，喜欢懒洋洋地靠在柜台后，对每位将要进来的客人的公民身份、国籍和地位发表长篇大论。

"天哪，这个郡真是块宝地，"他会这样说，徒劳地挥舞着一本小册子，像挥舞一块标语牌。他对心理学着了迷，会扯到微积分和平衡，仿佛人生的每个难题都能被细分并解决。举个例子，他坚称河边枫树的密度是源于某种宇宙法则，他带着滑稽的恐惧说出这个观点。他的论证会弯曲、滑脱，直到变得徒劳无功，恼人是真的，可他的态度又是那么和蔼，让你无法嘲笑他。

一个信使在三月的某个清晨到达，被派来给一群游客分配房间。一群青少年旅客开始在大堂里推搡、蹲坐，急切地想制定计划。其中一个得了腮腺炎；工作人员担心会传染给其他人，于是把那个小牛眼般的男孩隔开。他们的导游会为这一团人投保，并把行李托付给搬运工，他请店主帮大家熟悉环境。"你们得照看好自己的行李，"店主说，一边给每公斤行李贴上标签，客人们则互相交流、交换心得。

河上阳光与雨水的交替，赋予这地方一种任何歇业都抹不去的魅力。在一次水管漏水的小事故之后，店主没有屈尊去责怪任何人；他只是摆出一盘薄荷利口酒来款待客人，展示他的好客。路上的低洼、遮着门廊的大枫树、它那罕见而难得的宁静——所有这些构成了一幅我珍视的画面。没有任何背信，没有任何殉道者般的抱怨，能破坏那份记忆的留存。直到今天，这家简陋旅馆把一个疲惫灵魂送往安宁的能力，仍让我觉得近乎神圣。我和我的老伙伴每晚都会在门廊上烤鱼，看着一瓦微光闪烁；我学会了把账单分项、给每件东西贴标签，并毫无惧色地重复他教我的那句话。
